_meta.cycle_disposition_reason: Weekly bundle contains multiple new-this-cycle SA developments: FOMC hold April 29 under dual-mandate conflict, ECB hold April 30 with explicit stagflation risk acknowledgment, USTR launch of 76 new Section 301 investigations, and IMF WEO downgrade of EM growth to 3.9%. These constitute material regime-level changes across growth, inflation, trade, and policy domains.

lead_signal.headline: Federal Reserve holds rates at 3.50-3.75% on April 29 while USTR launches 76 new Section 301 investigations
lead_signal.body: The Federal Reserve maintained its policy rate unchanged on April 29, 2026, citing inflation above the 2% target and absent progress in recent months. Simultaneously, the USTR launched 76 new Section 301 investigations targeting China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, the EU, and other economies for forced labor enforcement and excess capacity. This dual development locks the Fed into a stagflationary policy trap: tariff-driven goods inflation prevents easing, while tariff-driven growth drag prevents tightening. The shift from IEEPA to Section 301 as the legal vehicle for tariffs represents a regime-level change in durability and scope, making this a structural rather than cyclical development.

module_0.title: Federal Reserve holds rates at 3.50-3.75% on April 29 while USTR launches 76 new Section 301 investigations
module_0.body: The Federal Reserve maintained its policy rate unchanged on April 29, 2026, citing inflation above the 2% target and absent progress in recent months. Simultaneously, the USTR launched 76 new Section 301 investigations targeting China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, the EU, and other economies for forced labor enforcement and excess capacity. This dual development locks the Fed into a stagflationary policy trap: tariff-driven goods inflation prevents easing, while tariff-driven growth drag prevents tightening. The shift from IEEPA to Section 301 as the legal vehicle for tariffs represents a regime-level change in durability and scope. Markets are pricing 2-3 Fed cuts in H2 2026; GMM assessment suggests zero cuts are analytically justified given the dual-mandate conflict. This is policy paralysis, not a neutral stance.

mainstream.headline: FOMC holds rates at 3.50-3.75% on April 29, 2026
mainstream.why_it_matters: The Federal Reserve maintained the federal funds rate target at 3.50-3.75% on April 29, 2026, with reserve balances remunerated at 3.65%. The hold follows the March 18 meeting which also held rates unchanged, with FOMC minutes noting that overall inflation remained above the Committee 2 percent longer-run goal and that further progress in reducing inflation had been absent in recent months. The Fed is caught between tariff-driven goods inflation and tariff-driven growth drag, a textbook stagflationary constraint that forecloses both easing and tightening as clean policy options. This is a policy paralysis signal, not a neutral stance signal.
mainstream.body: The Federal Reserve maintained the federal funds rate target at 3.50-3.75% on April 29, 2026, with reserve balances remunerated at 3.65%. The hold follows the March 18 meeting which also held rates unchanged, with FOMC minutes noting that overall inflation remained above the Committee 2 percent longer-run goal and that further progress in reducing inflation had been absent in recent months. The Fed is caught between tariff-driven goods inflation and tariff-driven growth drag, a textbook stagflationary constraint that forecloses both easing and tightening as clean policy options. This is a policy paralysis signal, not a neutral stance signal.
mainstream.asymmetric: Market is pricing approximately 2-3 Fed cuts in H2 2026 per futures curves; GMM assessment suggests zero cuts are analytically justified given inflation above target and tariff re-escalation via Section 301 adding further goods price pressure. Significant MATT divergence: market is too dovish on the Fed path.

mainstream.headline: ECB holds rates unchanged April 30, flags intensified stagflation risk from Middle East energy shock
mainstream.why_it_matters: The ECB Governing Council held all three key rates unchanged on April 30, 2026: deposit facility at 2.00%, main refinancing at 2.15%, marginal lending at 2.40%. The press release explicitly attributed the deterioration to the war in the Middle East, which has led to a sharp increase in energy prices, pushing up inflation and weighing on economic sentiment. ECB President Lagarde acknowledged the stagflation framing while resisting the label, noting 2026 growth projections of 0.9%, materially below prior estimates. A simultaneous intensification of both inflation upside and growth downside risks constitutes a regime-level shift in the ECB policy constraint set.
mainstream.body: The ECB Governing Council held all three key rates unchanged on April 30, 2026: deposit facility at 2.00%, main refinancing at 2.15%, marginal lending at 2.40%. The press release explicitly attributed the deterioration to the war in the Middle East, which has led to a sharp increase in energy prices, pushing up inflation and weighing on economic sentiment. ECB President Lagarde acknowledged the stagflation framing while resisting the label, noting 2026 growth projections of 0.9%, materially below prior estimates. A simultaneous intensification of both inflation upside and growth downside risks constitutes a regime-level shift in the ECB policy constraint set.
mainstream.asymmetric: Market may be pricing ECB rate hikes in H2 2026 given energy inflation; GMM assessment suggests the ECB is structurally constrained from hiking given 0.9% growth. The Transmission Protection Instrument reference in the press release signals the ECB is more concerned about fragmentation risk than inflation overshoot. Moderate MATT divergence on ECB hike probability.

mainstream.headline: IMF World Economic Outlook downgrades EM growth to 3.9% from 4.2%
mainstream.why_it_matters: The IMF World Economic Outlook released in April 2026 downgraded emerging market growth projections to 3.9% from 4.2%, citing the double hit from energy price increases and currency depreciation. The MECCA (Middle East, Central Asia, Caucasus, Afghanistan) region growth projection was cut to 1.9%. The IMF flagged that an adverse oil price scenario with Brent at 80 dollars per barrel higher than baseline could trigger a global contraction. This is a material downgrade that confirms EM economies are bearing the brunt of the dual energy and tariff shocks.
mainstream.body: The IMF World Economic Outlook released in April 2026 downgraded emerging market growth projections to 3.9% from 4.2%, citing the double hit from energy price increases and currency depreciation. The MECCA (Middle East, Central Asia, Caucasus, Afghanistan) region growth projection was cut to 1.9%. The IMF flagged that an adverse oil price scenario with Brent at 80 dollars per barrel higher than baseline could trigger a global contraction. This is a material downgrade that confirms EM economies are bearing the brunt of the dual energy and tariff shocks.
mainstream.asymmetric: The BIS USD debt loop amplifies EM stress: higher dollar funding costs plus weaker local currencies plus slower growth equals a sovereign debt sustainability crisis in the making. Gramercy EM high yield index down 0.50% in April confirms capital outflows are accelerating.

underweighted.headline: USTR launches 76 new Section 301 investigations, tariff re-escalation cycle enters durable legal phase
underweighted.why_it_matters: Following the US Supreme Court February 2026 ruling against IEEPA-based tariffs, the Trump administration launched 76 new Section 301 investigations targeting China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, the EU, and dozens of other economies: 60 for inadequate enforcement against forced labor and 16 for structural excess manufacturing capacity. PIIE analysis assesses this as a deliberate attempt to restore broad tariff coverage on a more legally durable basis, noting that Section 301 might be harder to dispose of through legal challenge than the prior two tariff assaults. Average US tariff rate now stands at approximately 12%, down from a 2025 peak of 28% but structurally elevated versus the pre-Trump baseline of 2.5%.
underweighted.body: Following the US Supreme Court February 2026 ruling against IEEPA-based tariffs, the Trump administration launched 76 new Section 301 investigations targeting China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, the EU, and dozens of other economies: 60 for inadequate enforcement against forced labor and 16 for structural excess manufacturing capacity. PIIE analysis assesses this as a deliberate attempt to restore broad tariff coverage on a more legally durable basis, noting that Section 301 might be harder to dispose of through legal challenge than the prior two tariff assaults. Average US tariff rate now stands at approximately 12%, down from a 2025 peak of 28% but structurally elevated versus the pre-Trump baseline of 2.5%.
underweighted.asymmetric: Market may be treating the post-Supreme Court period as a tariff de-escalation window; GMM assessment is the opposite. Section 301 investigations are a prelude to a more legally entrenched tariff regime. This is a significant MATT divergence: market is pricing de-escalation; GMM assesses re-escalation on a durable legal basis. The shift from IEEPA to Section 301 as the legal vehicle for tariffs represents a regime-level change in the durability and scope of US trade policy.

underweighted.headline: US-China trade decoupling deepens: US imports from China down 40% versus 2018 pre-war levels
underweighted.why_it_matters: PIIE analysis of 2025 US import data confirms that real US imports from China dropped 28% in 2025 alone and are now 40% below their 2018 pre-trade-war levels. China has actively diversified away from US agricultural exports: 80% of China soybean imports now come from Brazil and Argentina, up from 60% in 2017. The Trump administration November 2025 trade arrangement with China set future purchase commitments below the status quo, and US goods shipments to China fell to levels not seen since the 2008-09 global financial crisis. The decoupling is no longer reversible at the margin.
underweighted.body: PIIE analysis of 2025 US import data confirms that real US imports from China dropped 28% in 2025 alone and are now 40% below their 2018 pre-trade-war levels. China has actively diversified away from US agricultural exports: 80% of China soybean imports now come from Brazil and Argentina, up from 60% in 2017. The Trump administration November 2025 trade arrangement with China set future purchase commitments below the status quo, and US goods shipments to China fell to levels not seen since the 2008-09 global financial crisis. The decoupling is no longer reversible at the margin.
underweighted.asymmetric: A 40% structural decline in bilateral trade flows over a multi-year period, confirmed by official trade data, represents a permanent regime shift in global supply chains. This is not a cyclical trade war; it is a structural decoupling. The implications for EM exporters reliant on China final demand are severe. The implications for US inflation are also severe: loss of low-cost Chinese imports is a permanent upward shift in the US goods price level.

underweighted.headline: Strait of Hormuz flows remain fragile at 5-10 ships per day versus 138 normal, IEA flags most significant threat to global oil supply
underweighted.why_it_matters: Despite the April 2026 ceasefire, Strait of Hormuz shipping flows remain severely curtailed at 5-10 ships per day versus the normal 138 ships per day. The IEA flagged this as the most significant threat to global oil supply in its April Oil Market Report. Brent crude remains elevated at 104 dollars per barrel, down from a peak but still up 55% year-to-date. The IMF adverse oil price scenario assumes Brent at 80 dollars per barrel higher than baseline, which would trigger a global contraction. The ceasefire is tenuous and the supply shock is ongoing.
underweighted.body: Despite the April 2026 ceasefire, Strait of Hormuz shipping flows remain severely curtailed at 5-10 ships per day versus the normal 138 ships per day. The IEA flagged this as the most significant threat to global oil supply in its April Oil Market Report. Brent crude remains elevated at 104 dollars per barrel, down from a peak but still up 55% year-to-date. The IMF adverse oil price scenario assumes Brent at 80 dollars per barrel higher than baseline, which would trigger a global contraction. The ceasefire is tenuous and the supply shock is ongoing.
underweighted.asymmetric: The energy shock is the primary driver of the ECB stagflation constraint and the Fed inflation overshoot. It is also the primary driver of EM currency depreciation and capital outflows. The market is treating the ceasefire as a resolution; GMM assessment is that the ceasefire is fragile and the supply shock is structural until Hormuz flows normalize above 100 ships per day. This is a critical underweighted tail risk.

currency_signals.fx_stress_flags: FX stress is highest in EM currencies, where the dual energy and tariff shocks are hitting simultaneously. EM central banks are depleting reserves to defend currencies, which is a sovereign stress amplifier. The BIS USD debt loop is amplifying the stress: higher dollar funding costs plus weaker local currencies plus slower growth equals a structural tightening of global financial conditions. The dollar is strengthening as the Fed holds rates and EM stress intensifies, creating a self-reinforcing cycle.
currency_signals.dollar_dynamics: The dollar is strengthening as the Fed holds rates at 3.50-3.75% and EM stress intensifies. This is a structural tightening of global financial conditions. The BIS USD debt loop is amplifying EM sovereign debt stress: higher dollar funding costs plus weaker local currencies plus slower growth equals a sovereign debt sustainability crisis in the making. The dollar strength is also a transmission mechanism for the Fed policy paralysis: the Fed cannot ease due to inflation above 2%, and the strong dollar is exporting deflation to the rest of the world while importing inflation to the US via higher import prices.

sovereign_debt.debt_sustainability_flags: The most acute sovereign debt sustainability concerns this week are in EM economies hit by the dual energy and tariff shocks. The IMF downgrade of EM growth to 3.9% from 4.2%, combined with currency depreciation and capital outflows, is creating a sovereign stress amplifier. The BIS USD debt loop is amplifying the stress: higher dollar funding costs plus weaker local currencies plus slower growth equals a sovereign debt sustainability crisis in the making. The US Treasury maturity wall at 9.8 trillion dollars is a non-consensus tail risk that is underweighted by consensus.
sovereign_debt.imf_bis_signals: The IMF World Economic Outlook released in April 2026 downgraded EM growth projections to 3.9% from 4.2%, citing the double hit from energy price increases and currency depreciation. The IMF flagged that an adverse oil price scenario with Brent at 80 dollars per barrel higher than baseline could trigger a global contraction. The BIS flagged the USD debt loop as EM currencies depreciate and dollar funding costs rise, amplifying sovereign debt sustainability risk. These are regime-level signals that confirm the dual energy and tariff shocks are creating a structural tightening of global financial conditions.

vectors.name: Policy Rate Divergence
vectors.headline: Fed holds at 3.50-3.75%, ECB holds at 2.00%, PBOC holds at 3.10%, all cite dual-mandate conflicts
vectors.summary: The Fed, ECB, and PBOC all held rates unchanged in late April 2026, but for different reasons that collectively signal a global policy paralysis regime. The Fed cannot ease due to inflation above 2%, cannot tighten due to growth risk. The ECB cannot ease due to energy-driven inflation, cannot tighten due to 0.9% growth. The PBOC cannot ease due to currency stability concerns, cannot tighten due to growth drag from tariffs. This is a regime-level divergence in policy constraints, not a cyclical pause.
vectors.detail: The Fed, ECB, and PBOC all held rates unchanged in late April 2026, but for different reasons that collectively signal a global policy paralysis regime. The Fed cannot ease due to inflation above 2%, cannot tighten due to growth risk. The ECB cannot ease due to energy-driven inflation, cannot tighten due to 0.9% growth. The PBOC cannot ease due to currency stability concerns, cannot tighten due to growth drag from tariffs. This is a regime-level divergence in policy constraints, not a cyclical pause. The market is pricing 2-3 Fed cuts in H2 2026; GMM assessment suggests zero cuts are analytically justified. This is a significant MATT divergence.
vectors.asymmetric: Policy rate divergence is amplifying currency stress in EM and creating a structural tightening of global financial conditions. The BIS USD debt loop is amplifying EM sovereign debt stress.

The BIS USD debt loop is amplifying EM sovereign stress: higher dollar funding costs plus weaker local currencies plus slower growth equals a sovereign debt sustainability crisis in the making.

vectors.name: Private Credit Cascade
vectors.headline: Private credit gate events and CRE delinquency in fragility phase, real M2 negative
vectors.summary: Private credit gate events and CRE delinquency are in a fragility phase, with real M2 negative post-CPI 3.3%. The Fed hold at 3.50-3.75% suppresses CRE and residential real estate, with the 10-year yield at 4.35% and 1 trillion dollar annual interest expense. AI grid pockets provide minor offset but do not change the overall trajectory.

The Fed hold at 3.50-3.75% suppresses CRE and residential real estate, with the 10-year yield at 4.35% and 1 trillion dollar annual interest expense. AI grid pockets provide minor offset but do not change the overall trajectory. The reasoner analysis confirms Real Estate asset class at -0.82 BEARISH Deteriorating, with CRE fragility phase and liquidity peak as key drivers.
vectors.asymmetric: Private credit cascade risk is elevated but not yet high. The CRE fragility phase is a leading indicator of broader credit stress. Real M2 negative is a Blind Spot signal that is underweighted by consensus.

vectors.name: Banking Sector Stress
vectors.headline: Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide cushion but thinning, no acute stress signals in banking sector
vectors.summary: Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide cushion but are thinning. There are no acute stress signals in the banking sector this week. The Fed hold at 3.50-3.75% maintains the reserve remuneration rate at 3.65%. The Treasury liquidity WARNING is active but has not yet triggered acute banking sector stress.
vectors.detail: Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide cushion but are thinning. There are no acute stress signals in the banking sector this week. The Fed hold at 3.50-3.75% maintains the reserve remuneration rate at 3.65%. The Treasury liquidity WARNING is active but has not yet triggered acute banking sector stress. The reasoner analysis confirms no acute banking sector stress signals, but notes the Treasury liquidity crisis is a non-consensus tail risk.
vectors.asymmetric: Banking sector stress is moderate but not yet elevated. The Treasury liquidity crisis is a non-consensus tail risk that could trigger acute banking sector stress if the refinancing wall is not managed smoothly.

vectors.name: Trade Policy Shock
vectors.headline: USTR launches 76 new Section 301 investigations, average US tariff rate now 12%, US-China imports down 40%
vectors.summary: The USTR launched 76 new Section 301 investigations targeting China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, the EU, and dozens of other economies: 60 for inadequate enforcement against forced labor and 16 for structural excess manufacturing capacity. Average US tariff rate now stands at approximately 12%, down from a 2025 peak of 28% but structurally elevated versus the pre-Trump baseline of 2.5%. US imports from China are down 40% versus 2018 pre-trade-war levels.
vectors.detail: The USTR launched 76 new Section 301 investigations targeting China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, the EU, and dozens of other economies: 60 for inadequate enforcement against forced labor and 16 for structural excess manufacturing capacity. Average US tariff rate now stands at approximately 12%, down from a 2025 peak of 28% but structurally elevated versus the pre-Trump baseline of 2.5%. US imports from China are down 40% versus 2018 pre-trade-war levels. The shift from IEEPA to Section 301 as the legal vehicle for tariffs represents a regime-level change in the durability and scope of US trade policy. PIIE analysis assesses this as a deliberate attempt to restore broad tariff coverage on a more legally durable basis.
vectors.asymmetric: Trade policy shock is the primary driver of the Fed policy paralysis and the global stagflation regime. The shift from IEEPA to Section 301 represents a regime-level change in tariff durability. Market treating post-Supreme Court period as de-escalation; GMM assesses re-escalation on durable legal basis.

items.title: Treasury liquidity WARNING active: 9.8 trillion dollar maturity wall, 1 trillion dollar annual interest expense
items.summary: The US Treasury faces a 9.8 trillion dollar maturity wall with 1 trillion dollar annual interest expense. The 30-year auction was weak on April 9. Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide cushion but are thinning. The NY Fed notes war re-elevated volatility post-improvement. This is a non-consensus tail risk that is underweighted by consensus.
items.asymmetric: Treasury liquidity crisis is a non-consensus tail risk. Market is not pricing the refinancing wall adequately. If the refinancing wall is not managed smoothly, it could trigger acute banking sector stress and a broader financial system crisis.

items.title: FX swap basis volumes at 3.6 trillion dollars all-time high in Q1 2026
items.summary: FX swap basis volumes reached an all-time high of 3.6 trillion dollars in Q1 2026, with partial normalization on the ceasefire but stress intact. The reasoner analysis notes this is a TS spike not corroborated by SA signals, insufficient for regime shift but confirming ongoing dollar funding stress.
items.asymmetric: FX swap basis spike is a TS signal that is not corroborated by SA signals. However, it confirms ongoing dollar funding stress and is a leading indicator of potential liquidity fragmentation if SA signals deteriorate further.

items.title: Private credit gate events and CRE delinquency in fragility phase
items.summary: Private credit gate events and CRE delinquency are in a fragility phase, with real M2 negative post-CPI 3.3%. The Fed hold at 3.50-3.75% suppresses CRE and residential real estate, with the 10-year yield at 4.35% and 1 trillion dollar annual interest expense. AI grid pockets provide minor offset but do not change the overall trajectory.
items.asymmetric: Private credit cascade risk is elevated but not yet high. The CRE fragility phase is a leading indicator of broader credit stress. Real M2 negative is a Blind Spot signal that is underweighted by consensus.

module_9.title: Trade and Tariff Policy
module_9.digest_note: Tariff re-escalation cycle enters durable legal phase via Section 301 investigations

new_developments.title: USTR launches 76 new Section 301 investigations targeting China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, EU, and others
new_developments.actors: United States — China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, EU, and others
new_developments.summary: Following the US Supreme Court February 2026 ruling against IEEPA-based tariffs, the Trump administration launched 76 new Section 301 investigations: 60 for inadequate enforcement against forced labor and 16 for structural excess manufacturing capacity. PIIE analysis assesses this as a deliberate attempt to restore broad tariff coverage on a more legally durable basis, noting that Section 301 might be harder to dispose of through legal challenge than the prior two tariff assaults. Average US tariff rate now stands at approximately 12%, down from a 2025 peak of 28% but structurally elevated versus the pre-Trump baseline of 2.5%.
new_developments.enforcement: Investigations launched, tariff implementation pending completion of Section 301 process
new_developments.asymmetric: Shift from IEEPA to Section 301 represents regime-level change in tariff durability. Market treating post-Supreme Court period as de-escalation; GMM assesses re-escalation on durable legal basis. This is a significant MATT divergence.

new_developments.title: EU-US Turnberry tariff deal locks in 15% rate on EU exports
new_developments.summary: The Turnberry deal fixes US tariffs on EU exports at 15%, reducing uncertainty but eroding EU competitiveness as tariffs on rivals like China converge to similar levels. EU export growth to US projected to slow 4.6% in 2026 due to higher effective rates and relative disadvantage. This shifts trade drag from uncertainty to structural competitiveness loss. European Parliament endorsed the deal in plenary vote on April 19, but national ratification is pending.
new_developments.enforcement: European Parliament endorsed, national ratification pending
new_developments.asymmetric: Solidifies tariff shock as competitiveness drag rather than episodic uncertainty, upgrading EU-US trade friction to cyclical headwind. Locks in economic coercion phase of US-EU decoupling.

new_developments.title: US-China trade decoupling deepens: US imports from China down 40% versus 2018 pre-war levels
new_developments.summary: PIIE analysis of 2025 US import data confirms that real US imports from China dropped 28% in 2025 alone and are now 40% below their 2018 pre-trade-war levels. China has actively diversified away from US agricultural exports: 80% of China soybean imports now come from Brazil and Argentina, up from 60% in 2017. The Trump administration November 2025 trade arrangement with China set future purchase commitments below the status quo, and US goods shipments to China fell to levels not seen since the 2008-09 global financial crisis.
new_developments.asymmetric: A 40% structural decline in bilateral trade flows over a multi-year period represents a permanent regime shift in global supply chains. This is not a cyclical trade war; it is a structural decoupling. The implications for EM exporters reliant on China final demand are severe. The implications for US inflation are also severe: loss of low-cost Chinese imports is a permanent upward shift in the US goods price level.

no_change.item: EU steel and aluminum face sustained 25% and 10% US Section 232 tariffs
no_change.status: Ongoing Section 232 tariffs at 25% on steel, 10% on aluminum create high costs for EU auto, machinery, construction exporters
no_change.next_trigger: Negotiation progress on Section 232 or EU retaliation escalation

module_9.friction_analysis: Trade friction is highest in the US-China bilateral relationship, where the decoupling is now structural and irreversible at the margin. US imports from China are down 40% versus 2018 levels, and China has actively diversified away from US agricultural exports. The USTR launch of 76 new Section 301 investigations signals a durable tariff re-escalation cycle that is legally entrenched and harder to reverse than prior IEEPA-based tariffs. The EU-US Turnberry deal locks in a 15% tariff rate on EU exports, shifting trade drag from uncertainty to structural competitiveness loss. The friction is no longer episodic; it is regime-level.
module_9.tariff_escalation_rung: Tariff escalation is at Rung 3: enacted tariffs with active retaliation. The USTR launch of 76 new Section 301 investigations signals a move toward Rung 4: broad-based tariff regime with legal durability. The shift from IEEPA to Section 301 as the legal vehicle for tariffs represents a regime-level change in the durability and scope of US trade policy. The critical path is the completion of the Section 301 investigations and the implementation of new tariffs, which would lock in a structurally elevated tariff regime for years. The market is treating the post-Supreme Court period as a de-escalation window; GMM assesses re-escalation on a durable legal basis.

retaliation_chain.measure: Diversification away from US agricultural exports: 80% of soybean imports now from Brazil and Argentina
retaliation_chain.macro_impact: Permanent loss of US agricultural export market share, structural decoupling of US-China trade

retaliation_chain.measure: Retaliatory duties on US agricultural products
retaliation_chain.macro_impact: Modest impact on US agricultural exports, but signals EU willingness to retaliate

multilateral_signals.item: IMF World Economic Outlook downgrades EM growth to 3.9%, flags adverse oil scenario risk
multilateral_signals.summary: The IMF World Economic Outlook released in April 2026 downgraded emerging market growth projections to 3.9% from 4.2%, citing the double hit from energy price increases and currency depreciation. The MECCA region growth projection was cut to 1.9%. The IMF flagged that an adverse oil price scenario with Brent at 80 dollars per barrel higher than baseline could trigger a global contraction.
multilateral_signals.asymmetric: Material downgrade confirms EM economies bearing brunt of dual energy and tariff shocks. BIS USD debt loop amplifies sovereign stress. This is a regime-level signal that confirms the global stagflation regime.

multilateral_signals.item: IEA flags Strait of Hormuz as most significant threat to global oil supply
multilateral_signals.summary: The IEA flagged the Strait of Hormuz as the most significant threat to global oil supply in its April Oil Market Report. Despite the April 2026 ceasefire, shipping flows remain severely curtailed at 5-10 ships per day versus the normal 138 ships per day. Brent crude remains elevated at 104 dollars per barrel, down from a peak but still up 55% year-to-date.
multilateral_signals.asymmetric: Ceasefire is tenuous; supply shock is structural until Hormuz flows normalize above 100 ships per day. Critical underweighted tail risk that is driving the global stagflation regime.

multilateral_signals.item: BIS flags USD debt loop as EM currencies depreciate and dollar funding costs rise
multilateral_signals.summary: The BIS flagged the USD debt loop as EM currencies depreciate and dollar funding costs rise, amplifying sovereign debt sustainability risk. Cross-border USD lending to EM is reversing as the Fed holds rates and the dollar strengthens. This is a structural tightening of global financial conditions.
multilateral_signals.asymmetric: BIS USD debt loop amplifies EM sovereign stress. This is a structural tightening of global financial conditions that is underweighted by consensus. The implications for EM sovereign debt sustainability are severe.

central_bank_coordination.item: Fed, ECB, and PBOC all hold rates unchanged in late April 2026, but for different reasons signalling global policy paralysis
central_bank_coordination.institutions: Federal Reserve, European Central Bank, People's Bank of China
central_bank_coordination.summary: The Fed, ECB, and PBOC all held rates unchanged in late April 2026, but for different reasons that collectively signal a global policy paralysis regime. The Fed cannot ease due to inflation above 2%, cannot tighten due to growth risk. The ECB cannot ease due to energy-driven inflation, cannot tighten due to 0.9% growth. The PBOC cannot ease due to currency stability concerns, cannot tighten due to growth drag from tariffs. This is a regime-level divergence in policy constraints, not a cyclical pause.

fiscal_governance.item: US Treasury maturity wall at 9.8 trillion dollars, annual interest expense 1 trillion dollars
fiscal_governance.summary: The US Treasury faces a 9.8 trillion dollar maturity wall with 1 trillion dollar annual interest expense. The 30-year auction was weak on April 9. Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide cushion but are thinning. The NY Fed notes war re-elevated volatility post-improvement. This is a non-consensus tail risk that is underweighted by consensus.

governance_gaps.gap: Lack of multilateral coordination on tariff de-escalation
governance_gaps.analysis: The USTR launch of 76 new Section 301 investigations signals a durable tariff re-escalation cycle that is legally entrenched and harder to reverse than prior IEEPA-based tariffs. There is no multilateral coordination mechanism to de-escalate the tariff regime. The WTO dispute resolution mechanism is effectively non-functional. The G7 and G20 have not produced any coordinated response to the tariff escalation.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: If unaddressed, the tariff re-escalation cycle will lock in a structurally elevated tariff regime for years, creating a permanent upward shift in the US goods price level and a permanent loss of low-cost Chinese imports. This is a regime-level governance gap that is underweighted by consensus.

governance_gaps.gap: Lack of multilateral coordination on energy supply shock response
governance_gaps.analysis: The Strait of Hormuz shipping flows remain severely curtailed at 5-10 ships per day versus the normal 138 ships per day. The IEA flagged this as the most significant threat to global oil supply. There is no multilateral coordination mechanism to address the energy supply shock. The ceasefire is tenuous and the supply shock is ongoing.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: If unaddressed, the energy supply shock will continue to drive the global stagflation regime, with the ECB and Fed both caught in a policy paralysis trap. The IMF adverse oil price scenario assumes Brent at 80 dollars per barrel higher than baseline, which would trigger a global contraction. This is a regime-level governance gap that is underweighted by consensus.

module_11.title: Scenario and Tail Risk

items.title: Tariff re-escalation to pharma 100% in September 2026 plus Section 122 expiry in July exposing 9.7% baseline
items.probability_delta: Likelihood increased to 0.75 from 0.65 given USTR launch of 76 new Section 301 investigations
items.asymmetric: Rung 3 active with China, EU, Canada, Mexico retaliation; SCOTUS recalibration temporary, pharma tariff locked. Yale Budget Lab confirms path to higher rates. ERM commodity chain stress multiplier. If this scenario materializes, the Fed policy paralysis trap deepens and the global stagflation regime intensifies.

items.title: Treasury liquidity crisis on 9.8 trillion dollar maturity wall plus Iran volatility
items.probability_delta: Likelihood stable at 0.65, impact increased to 0.90 given weak 30-year auction April 9
items.asymmetric: WARNING active; NY Fed notes war re-elevated volatility post-improvement. 800 billion dollars per month refinancing versus ample but finite reserves equals non-consensus snap risk not in sell-side decks. If this scenario materializes, it could trigger acute banking sector stress and a broader financial system crisis.

items.title: Hormuz full re-closure on ceasefire failure
items.probability_delta: Likelihood stable at 0.55, impact stable at 0.95
items.asymmetric: Brent 104 dollars but 115-125 dollars Q2 average triggers global contraction per Oxford. Iran IRGC permission requirement signals escalation path. IEA flags most significant threat. If this scenario materializes, the global stagflation regime intensifies and the ECB and Fed policy paralysis traps deepen.

structural_trends.title: US-China structural decoupling
structural_trends.trend: US imports from China down 40% versus 2018 pre-trade-war levels. China has actively diversified away from US agricultural exports. The decoupling is no longer reversible at the margin. This is a permanent regime shift in global supply chains.

structural_trends.title: Global stagflation regime
structural_trends.trend: The dual energy and tariff shocks are creating a global stagflation regime. Central banks are caught in a policy paralysis trap: cannot ease due to inflation, cannot tighten due to growth risk. This is a regime-level shift, not a cyclical pause.

structural_trends.title: EM sovereign debt sustainability crisis
structural_trends.trend: The BIS USD debt loop is amplifying EM sovereign stress: higher dollar funding costs plus weaker local currencies plus slower growth equals a sovereign debt sustainability crisis in the making. The IMF downgrade of EM growth to 3.9% confirms the stress is structural.

asymmetric_flags: Market is pricing 2-3 Fed cuts in H2 2026; GMM assessment suggests zero cuts are analytically justified given inflation above target and tariff re-escalation via Section 301 adding further goods price pressure. Significant MATT divergence: market is too dovish on the Fed path.

Market may be treating the post-Supreme Court period as a tariff de-escalation window; GMM assessment is the opposite. Section 301 investigations are a prelude to a more legally entrenched tariff regime. This is a significant MATT divergence: market is pricing de-escalation; GMM assesses re-escalation on a durable legal basis.

The energy shock is the primary driver of the ECB stagflation constraint and the Fed inflation overshoot. The market is treating the ceasefire as a resolution; GMM assessment is that the ceasefire is fragile and the supply shock is structural until Hormuz flows normalize above 100 ships per day. This is a critical underweighted tail risk.

Treasury liquidity crisis is a non-consensus tail risk. Market is not pricing the refinancing wall adequately. If the refinancing wall is not managed smoothly, it could trigger acute banking sector stress and a broader financial system crisis.

macro_health_composite.note: Macro health composite deteriorates to 0.32 from prior 0.40 as dual energy and tariff shocks create global stagflation regime; central banks in policy paralysis trap

key_judgments.judgment: The Federal Reserve is locked into a stagflationary policy trap: tariff-driven goods inflation prevents easing, while tariff-driven growth drag prevents tightening. This is policy paralysis, not a neutral stance. Market pricing of 2-3 Fed cuts in H2 2026 is analytically unjustified.

The USTR launch of 76 new Section 301 investigations signals a durable tariff re-escalation cycle that is legally entrenched and harder to reverse than prior IEEPA-based tariffs. The shift from IEEPA to Section 301 represents a regime-level change in the durability and scope of US trade policy. Market is treating the post-Supreme Court period as a tariff de-escalation window; GMM assesses re-escalation on a durable legal basis.

The ECB is caught in a stagflationary policy trap: energy-driven inflation prevents easing, while 0.9% growth projection prevents tightening. The simultaneous intensification of inflation upside and growth downside risks is a regime-level shift in the ECB policy constraint set. Market may be pricing ECB rate hikes in H2 2026; GMM assessment suggests the ECB is structurally constrained from hiking.

The Strait of Hormuz energy supply shock is structural, not episodic. Shipping flows remain severely curtailed at 5-10 ships per day versus the normal 138 ships per day. The ceasefire is tenuous and the supply shock is ongoing. The market is treating the ceasefire as a resolution; GMM assessment is that the ceasefire is fragile and the supply shock is structural until Hormuz flows normalize above 100 ships per day. This is a critical underweighted tail risk.

The BIS USD debt loop is amplifying EM sovereign stress: higher dollar funding costs plus weaker local currencies plus slower growth equals a sovereign debt sustainability crisis in the making. The IMF downgrade of EM growth to 3.9% from 4.2% confirms the stress is structural. EM capital outflows are accelerating, with the Gramercy EM high yield index down 0.50% in April.

The US-China trade decoupling is now structural and irreversible at the margin. US imports from China are down 40% versus 2018 pre-trade-war levels, and China has actively diversified away from US agricultural exports. This is a permanent regime shift in global supply chains. The implications for EM exporters reliant on China final demand are severe. The implications for US inflation are also severe: loss of low-cost Chinese imports is a permanent upward shift in the US goods price level.

The US Treasury maturity wall at 9.8 trillion dollars with 1 trillion dollar annual interest expense is a non-consensus tail risk that is underweighted by consensus. The 30-year auction was weak on April 9. Bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars provide cushion but are thinning. If the refinancing wall is not managed smoothly, it could trigger acute banking sector stress and a broader financial system crisis.

proposed_patches.rationale: EM sovereign spreads widening as IMF downgrades growth, capital outflows accelerate, and BIS USD debt loop amplifies stress. Sovereign debt sustainability crisis in the making.

proposed_patches.rule_applied: Currency Regime Stress risk vector rating change
proposed_patches.material_change_justification: EM currencies depreciating amid energy shock and capital outflows, dollar strengthening as Fed holds rates. Rating upgraded from ELEVATED to HIGH.
proposed_patches.rationale: EM central banks depleting reserves to defend currencies. BIS USD debt loop amplifying stress: higher dollar funding costs plus weaker local currencies plus slower growth equals structural tightening of global financial conditions.

proposed_patches.rule_applied: Trade Policy Shock risk vector rating change
proposed_patches.material_change_justification: USTR launches 76 new Section 301 investigations, average US tariff rate now 12%, US-China imports down 40%. Rating upgraded from ELEVATED to HIGH.
proposed_patches.rationale: Shift from IEEPA to Section 301 represents regime-level change in tariff durability. Market treating post-Supreme Court period as de-escalation; GMM assesses re-escalation on durable legal basis.

proposed_patches.material_change_justification: FOMC hold April 29 plus 76 new Section 301 probes reinforces stagflationary trap within CRISIS regime. Conviction remains VERY HIGH.

value.rationale: FOMC hold April 29 plus 76 new Section 301 probes reinforces stagflationary trap within CRISIS regime; three-indicator corroboration met; no SA movement toward RECESSION or STAGFLATION alone

proposed_patches.rationale: Weekly lead signal confirms policy paralysis and tariff re-escalation as SA developments reinforcing CRISIS regime. System average stable at negative 0.686.

proposed_patches.material_change_justification: USTR launch of 76 new Section 301 investigations triggers new tactical alert for Section 301 Tariff Re-Escalation.

value.name: Treasury Liquidity WARNING
value.description: 9.8 trillion dollar maturity wall plus 1 trillion dollar annual interest expense versus ample but finite bank reserves at 3.2 trillion dollars equals non-consensus snap risk. 30-year auction weak April 9. NY Fed notes war re-elevated volatility post-improvement.

value.name: Hormuz Energy Supply Shock
value.description: Strait of Hormuz shipping flows remain severely curtailed at 5-10 ships per day versus normal 138 ships per day. IEA flags most significant threat to global oil supply. Brent crude 104 dollars per barrel, up 55% year-to-date. Ceasefire tenuous.

value.name: Section 301 Tariff Re-Escalation
value.description: USTR launched 76 new Section 301 investigations targeting China, Vietnam, Taiwan, Mexico, Japan, EU, and others. Shift from IEEPA to Section 301 represents regime-level change in tariff durability. Average US tariff rate now 12%, structurally elevated versus 2.5% baseline.

proposed_patches.rationale: New tactical alert added for Section 301 Tariff Re-Escalation given USTR launch of 76 investigations and regime-level change in tariff durability.
